description:
Road rules are laws that all road users must follow.

Drivers must obey traffic signs, traffic lights, road markings, and road rules at all times.

Police officers and authorised traffic controllers have legal authority to direct traffic.

If a police officer or authorised traffic controller gives a direction that conflicts with signs or traffic lights, the direction must be followed.

Failing to obey a lawful direction is an offence.

Drivers must drive with due care and attention at all times.

A driver must not drive in a manner that is careless, dangerous, or negligent.

Drivers must take reasonable steps to avoid crashes and injury.

A driver is legally responsible for their actions and the safe operation of their vehicle.

Endangering other road users is a breach of road law.

Giving way means slowing down and stopping if necessary to avoid a collision.

Drivers must give way when required by signs, road rules, or traffic conditions.

A driver entering a road from a driveway or parking position must give way to all traffic.

When changing lanes, the driver changing lanes must give way to other vehicles.

Failing to give way when required is an offence.

An uncontrolled intersection has no traffic lights or signs.

At an uncontrolled intersection, drivers must give way to vehicles approaching from the right.

At a T-intersection without signs, the driver on the terminating road must give way to all traffic on the continuing road.

Drivers must approach intersections with caution and be prepared to give way.

Drivers must use indicators to signal their intentions.

Indicators must be used when turning, changing lanes, merging, or moving in or out of a parking position.

Indicators must be given for long enough to warn other road users.

Failing to signal correctly can cause crashes and is an offence.

Drivers must not enter an intersection unless there is enough space to clear it.

Blocking an intersection can obstruct traffic and emergency vehicles.

Even with a green light, drivers must not proceed if their path is blocked.

Drivers are responsible for ensuring they can fully exit the intersection.

Temporary signs and directions in roadwork areas must be obeyed.

Drivers must follow reduced speed limits and directions from authorised traffic controllers.

Roadwork zones may contain workers, machinery, changed traffic conditions, or uneven surfaces.

Failing to obey temporary road rules can result in fines and penalties.

Drivers are legally responsible for complying with all road rules.

A driver must ensure their vehicle is safe and roadworthy before driving.

Breaking road laws may result in fines, demerit points, licence suspension, or court penalties.

Safe and lawful driving helps protect all road users.

All road users must act safely and cooperate with each other.

Drivers should remain patient, courteous, and aware of vulnerable road users.

Aggressive or inconsiderate driving can increase crash risk and may be unlawful.

Safe cooperation helps traffic flow smoothly and reduces danger on the road.

Drivers must make safe and lawful decisions in all traffic situations.

Before acting, drivers should assess traffic, road conditions, visibility, and potential hazards.

Unsafe decisions can place drivers, passengers, and other road users at risk.

Careful decision making is an important part of responsible driving.

Regulatory signs inform drivers of laws and road rules that must be followed.

Examples include STOP, GIVE WAY, NO ENTRY, and TURN RESTRICTION signs.

Drivers must obey regulatory signs at all times unless directed otherwise by police or authorised traffic controllers.

Failing to obey a regulatory sign is an offence.

Speed signs display the maximum legal speed allowed on a road.

Drivers must not exceed the posted speed limit.

Speed limits may change in school zones, roadwork areas, or due to electronic signs.

Driving too fast for the conditions is dangerous even if within the speed limit.

Warning signs alert drivers to hazards or changing road conditions ahead.

Examples include curves, intersections, pedestrian crossings, and wildlife warning signs.

Drivers should slow down and prepare to respond safely when approaching warning signs.

Ignoring warning signs can increase crash risk.

Traffic lights control the movement of vehicles and pedestrians at intersections.

Drivers must stop at a red light and only proceed when the light turns green.

A yellow light means stop safely if possible before entering the intersection.

Drivers must not enter an intersection if their path is blocked, even with a green light.

Pedestrian signals control when pedestrians may cross the road.

Drivers must give way to pedestrians lawfully crossing at crossings or intersections.

A flashing red pedestrian signal means pedestrians must finish crossing but not start crossing.

Drivers should remain alert for pedestrians near crossings and intersections.

Temporary and roadwork signs provide instructions and warnings for changed traffic conditions.

Drivers must obey temporary speed limits, lane closures, and directions from traffic controllers.

Roadwork areas may contain workers, machinery, or uneven road surfaces.

Extra caution is required when travelling through roadwork zones.

Lane control signals show whether a lane is open, closed, or restricted.

A green arrow means drivers may use the lane.

A red X means drivers must not drive in that lane.

Drivers must obey lane control signals and move safely when lanes are closed.

Railway signals warn drivers of approaching trains and railway crossings.

Drivers must stop when warning lights flash, bells sound, or boom gates are lowering or down.

Drivers must not enter a railway crossing unless there is enough room to clear it safely.

Ignoring railway signals is extremely dangerous and unlawful.

Advisory and guide signs provide information about routes, destinations, services, and road conditions.

These signs help drivers navigate safely and plan ahead.

Advisory speed signs recommend a safer speed for conditions such as curves or ramps.

Drivers should consider advisory information to improve safety.

Drivers must obey all traffic signs, signals, and road markings.

Traffic signals are designed to manage traffic safely and efficiently.

If traffic lights are not working, drivers must follow the applicable road rules and give way requirements.

Police officers and authorised traffic controllers may override traffic signals with lawful directions.

Speed limits are the maximum legal speeds allowed on roads.

Drivers must obey posted speed limits at all times.

Driving above the speed limit is illegal and increases crash risk.

Drivers must also reduce speed when conditions make the posted limit unsafe.

School zones operate during the times shown on school zone signs.

Drivers must slow to the posted school zone speed limit when the zone is active.

Extra care is required because children may behave unpredictably near roads.

Failing to obey school zone speed limits can result in serious penalties.

Shared zones are areas where pedestrians and vehicles share the road space.

Drivers must travel at or below the posted shared zone speed limit.

Pedestrians have priority in shared zones.

Drivers must be prepared to stop safely at any time.

Temporary speed limits apply in roadwork and construction zones.

Drivers must obey all temporary speed signs and directions from traffic controllers.

Roadwork areas may contain workers, machinery, lane changes, or uneven surfaces.

Extra caution is required when travelling through work zones.

Drivers must adjust speed to suit weather and road conditions.

Rain, fog, wind, and poor visibility can increase stopping distances and reduce control.

Driving too fast for conditions is dangerous even if within the speed limit.

Drivers should slow down and leave more space in poor weather.

Drivers must leave enough distance behind the vehicle ahead to stop safely.

A safe following distance gives drivers more time to react to hazards.

Following too closely, also known as tailgating, increases crash risk.

Larger gaps are needed in wet weather or when driving at higher speeds.

Stopping distance is the total distance travelled before a vehicle comes to a complete stop.

Stopping distance includes reaction distance and braking distance.

Higher speeds increase the distance required to stop safely.

Wet or slippery roads can greatly increase stopping distances.

Safe driving requires choosing a speed suitable for the conditions.

Drivers should consider traffic, road conditions, visibility, and weather when selecting speed.

Driving at a safe speed improves vehicle control and reduces crash severity.

Drivers must always be able to stop safely within the distance visible ahead.

Drivers must obey road rules and drive safely at all times.

A driver is responsible for controlling the vehicle and avoiding danger to others.

Drivers must follow traffic signs, signals, road markings, and lawful directions.

Unsafe or unlawful driving can result in penalties and may place other road users at risk.

Drivers involved in a crash must stop and provide assistance where required.

Drivers may need to exchange details with other people involved.

Police must be contacted in certain crash situations, such as when someone is injured or a vehicle cannot be safely driven.

Leaving a crash scene without meeting legal responsibilities is an offence.

Drivers and passengers must wear seatbelts where fitted.

Drivers are responsible for ensuring children are properly restrained in approved child restraints or seatbelts.

Child restraints must suit the child's age and size.

Seatbelts and restraints reduce the risk of serious injury in a crash.

Using a mobile phone or device while driving can distract the driver and increase crash risk.

Drivers must follow the rules for phone and device use that apply to their licence type.

Learner and provisional drivers are usually subject to stricter mobile phone rules.

A driver should avoid distractions and keep full attention on the road.

Drivers must ensure their vehicle is safe and roadworthy before driving.

Important vehicle parts include brakes, tyres, lights, indicators, mirrors, and steering.

Driving a defective or unsafe vehicle can lead to penalties and increase crash risk.

Regular checks and maintenance help keep the vehicle safe.

Fatigue reduces concentration, reaction time, and decision making.

Drivers should not drive when tired or sleepy.

Taking rest breaks, sharing driving, and avoiding long periods without sleep can reduce fatigue risk.

If a driver feels drowsy, the safest choice is to stop and rest.

Learner and provisional drivers must follow the conditions that apply to their licence.

These conditions may include displaying plates, obeying special speed or passenger rules, and following stricter mobile phone and alcohol rules.

Licence conditions are legal requirements, not optional guidelines.

Breaking learner or provisional conditions can result in fines, demerit points, or licence action.

A supervising driver must meet the legal requirements to supervise a learner driver.

The supervisor must be able to guide the learner and help them drive safely.

A supervising driver must not be affected by alcohol, drugs, fatigue, or anything else that could impair supervision.

Good supervision helps learners develop safe driving habits.

Blood Alcohol Concentration, or BAC, measures the amount of alcohol in a person's bloodstream.

Alcohol affects judgement, reaction time, coordination, and driving ability.

Learner and provisional drivers are generally required to maintain zero BAC while driving.

Driving with an illegal BAC can result in severe penalties and increased crash risk.

Police may conduct random breath testing to detect drink driving.

Drivers must stop and provide a breath sample when lawfully directed.

Refusing to take a breath test is an offence.

Random breath testing helps improve road safety and reduce alcohol-related crashes.

Illegal drugs, some prescription medicines, and certain over-the-counter medicines can impair driving ability.

Drivers must not drive while affected by drugs or medication.

Some medicines may cause drowsiness, slower reactions, or poor concentration.

Drivers should read medicine warnings and seek advice if unsure about driving safely.

Fatigue can seriously affect concentration, reaction time, and decision making.

Driving while tired increases the risk of falling asleep at the wheel.

Long trips, lack of sleep, and driving at night can increase fatigue risk.

Drivers should take regular breaks and avoid driving when sleepy.

Some medical conditions can affect a person's ability to drive safely.

Conditions affecting vision, movement, awareness, or concentration may impact driving.

Drivers may be required to report certain medical conditions to the licensing authority.

Medical advice should be followed to ensure safe driving.

Drivers must be physically and mentally fit to drive safely.

Illness, fatigue, alcohol, drugs, stress, or emotional distraction can reduce driving ability.

A driver should not drive if they are impaired or unable to concentrate fully.

Being fit to drive helps protect the driver, passengers, and other road users.

Hazard perception involves identifying and responding to potential dangers early.

Drivers should constantly scan the road ahead, mirrors, and surrounding traffic.

Good hazard perception provides more time to react safely.

Anticipating hazards helps reduce the risk of crashes.

A space cushion is the safe distance maintained around a vehicle.

Leaving space in front, behind, and beside the vehicle improves safety.

A larger space cushion provides more time to react to hazards.

Drivers should increase space in poor weather or heavy traffic.

Drivers must choose a speed that suits road, traffic, and weather conditions.

Driving at a safe speed improves control and reduces stopping distance.

The posted speed limit is the maximum legal speed, not always the safest speed.

Drivers should slow down when visibility or road conditions are poor.

Wet roads reduce tyre grip and increase stopping distances.

Drivers should slow down, leave extra following distance, and avoid sudden braking.

Headlights should be used when visibility is reduced.

Careful driving in wet weather helps prevent skidding and loss of control.

Night driving reduces visibility and can make hazards harder to detect.

Drivers should use headlights correctly and watch carefully for pedestrians, cyclists, and animals.

Fatigue is more common during night driving.

Drivers should slow down and remain alert when driving at night.

Drivers should maintain a safe following distance from the vehicle ahead.

Smooth and controlled braking helps maintain vehicle stability.

Following too closely increases the risk of rear-end crashes.

Extra braking distance is required at higher speeds and in wet conditions.

Defensive driving means anticipating hazards and preparing for the actions of other road users.

Drivers should remain alert, patient, and aware of changing conditions.

Safe defensive driving includes leaving space, scanning ahead, and avoiding unnecessary risks.

Defensive driving helps reduce crash risk and improves road safety.

Intersections are high-risk areas where drivers must be especially alert.

Drivers should approach intersections at a safe speed and be prepared to give way.

Checking for vehicles, pedestrians, and cyclists is essential before proceeding.

Careful observation helps prevent intersection crashes.

Animals, loose gravel, potholes, and slippery surfaces can create hazards for drivers.

Drivers should reduce speed and remain alert in areas where hazards may appear.

Sudden steering or braking can cause loss of control.

Adjusting driving to suit road conditions improves safety.

Drivers should adjust the seat and mirrors before driving.

A correct seating position improves control, comfort, and visibility.

Mirrors should provide the clearest possible view around the vehicle.

Proper adjustment helps reduce blind spots and improves awareness of traffic.

Drivers must give way according to road rules, signs, signals, and traffic conditions.

At intersections, drivers should slow down and check carefully for other vehicles, pedestrians, and cyclists.

Failing to give way can lead to serious crashes.

Drivers must be prepared to stop if necessary to avoid a collision.

Drivers must turn from and into the correct lane where required.

Indicators must be used before turning.

Drivers must check for pedestrians, cyclists, and other vehicles before completing a turn.

Turning safely requires good observation and correct positioning.

Slip lanes allow vehicles to turn without entering the main intersection.

Drivers using a slip lane must give way to pedestrians crossing the slip lane.

Drivers should approach slip lanes carefully and maintain safe speed.

Observation is important because pedestrians and cyclists may be difficult to see.

Drivers approaching a roundabout must give way to vehicles already in the roundabout.

Drivers should choose the correct lane before entering.

Indicators are used when exiting a roundabout where required.

Drivers should travel at a safe speed and remain alert for other road users.

Drivers must not enter an intersection unless there is enough space to clear it completely.

Blocking intersections can obstruct traffic flow and emergency vehicles.

Even with a green light, drivers must wait if traffic ahead prevents safe exit.

Drivers should plan ahead before entering busy intersections.

Drivers must give way to pedestrians crossing roads at intersections when required.

Extra care is needed near crossings, schools, and busy pedestrian areas.

Pedestrians may enter crossings unexpectedly.

Drivers should slow down and remain alert around intersections.

Drivers turning right must give way to oncoming traffic and pedestrians where required.

Right turns should be made from the correct lane and with proper signalling.

Drivers should not begin turning until it is safe to do so.

Careful observation is essential when turning right across traffic.

Drivers turning left must signal and position the vehicle correctly before turning.

Drivers must check for pedestrians, cyclists, and vehicles before completing the turn.

Left turns should be made carefully and at a safe speed.

Observation and lane positioning are important for safe left turns.

Roundabouts can present hazards such as incorrect lane use, sudden lane changes, and failure to give way.

Drivers should remain alert for cyclists, pedestrians, and large vehicles.

Entering too quickly can reduce vehicle control.

Safe roundabout driving requires patience and careful observation.

Drivers should approach all intersections with caution.

Checking traffic from all directions helps identify potential hazards early.

Intersections are common locations for crashes because multiple road users interact.

Safe speeds, observation, and correct giving way are essential for intersection safety.

Lane markings help organise traffic and guide drivers safely on the road.

Drivers must obey continuous lines, broken lines, arrows, and painted lane markings.

Crossing certain lane lines may be prohibited except in specific situations.

Road markings improve traffic flow and reduce crash risk.

Drivers should use the correct lane for their direction and type of travel.

Some lanes may be reserved for turning, buses, bicycles, or high occupancy vehicles.

Drivers must obey lane arrows and lane control signs.

Using lanes correctly improves traffic flow and safety.

Drivers must only overtake when it is safe and legal to do so.

Before overtaking, drivers should check mirrors, blind spots, and oncoming traffic.

Overtaking is prohibited in certain areas, including where signs or continuous lines forbid it.

Drivers must return safely to their lane after overtaking.

Merging occurs when two lanes combine into one.

Drivers should merge smoothly and safely without forcing other vehicles to brake suddenly.

When lanes merge and no lane lines are marked, drivers must give way to vehicles ahead.

Careful observation and signalling help safe merging.

Special lanes include bus lanes, bicycle lanes, transit lanes, and turning lanes.

Only permitted vehicles may use certain special lanes.

Drivers must obey all signs and lane markings relating to special lanes.

Improper use of special lanes can result in penalties.

On multi-lane roads with higher speed limits, drivers may be required to keep left unless overtaking.

Keeping left helps traffic move safely and efficiently.

Drivers should avoid unnecessarily staying in the right lane.

Road signs may indicate when keep left rules apply.

Drivers changing lanes must give way to vehicles already in the lane.

Before changing lanes, drivers should check mirrors, blind spots, and signal appropriately.

Lane changes should be made smoothly and only when safe.

Unsafe lane changes can cause collisions and disrupt traffic flow.

Safe overtaking requires clear visibility, adequate space, and good judgement.

Drivers should avoid overtaking near intersections, curves, crests, or pedestrian crossings.

Overtaking should be completed efficiently without exceeding safe speeds.

Drivers must ensure they can return safely to their lane.

Drivers should remain aware of surrounding traffic and maintain safe lane positioning.

Correct lane use reduces confusion and improves road safety.

Drivers must avoid sudden movements that may surprise other road users.

Observation and planning are important for safe lane driving.

Safe driving requires observation, planning, patience, and good judgement.

Drivers should anticipate hazards and adjust driving to suit conditions.

Maintaining safe distances and obeying road rules helps reduce crash risk.

Responsible driving protects all road users.

Stopping and parking have different meanings under road rules.

A vehicle is considered parked when left stationary, even briefly, unless actively picking up or dropping off passengers.

Drivers must obey all signs and restrictions relating to stopping and parking.

Illegal stopping or parking can obstruct traffic and create hazards.

Drivers must not stop in a no stopping zone unless required by traffic conditions or emergencies.

No stopping signs are designed to keep traffic moving safely.

Stopping briefly to pick up or drop off passengers is not allowed in these zones.

Breaching no stopping restrictions can result in penalties.

Drivers may stop briefly in a no parking zone only for permitted passenger pick-up or drop-off.

The driver must remain near the vehicle and move it promptly when required.

Longer stopping or leaving the vehicle unattended is not permitted.

No parking restrictions help maintain traffic flow and safety.

Stopping or parking is not permitted in a clearway during the times shown on signs.

Clearways are designed to improve traffic flow during busy periods.

Vehicles parked illegally in clearways may be fined or towed away.

Drivers should check clearway signs carefully before parking.

Drivers must not stop or park in bus zones unless permitted by signs.

Bus stops and zones are reserved to allow buses to pick up and drop off passengers safely.

Stopping in bus areas can obstruct public transport and create hazards.

Drivers should remain alert for buses entering or leaving bus zones.

Drivers must not stop or park across driveways or too close to intersections.

Parking restrictions near intersections improve visibility and safety for all road users.

Blocking driveways can prevent access for residents and emergency services.

Drivers should always check signs and road markings before parking.

Loading zones are reserved for vehicles permitted by the sign.

These zones are intended for loading or unloading goods and passengers where allowed.

Drivers must follow all conditions displayed on loading zone signs.

Improper use of loading zones may result in fines or penalties.

Vehicles must be parked safely and within marked parking spaces where provided.

Drivers should park close to the kerb and avoid obstructing traffic.

Parking on footpaths, nature strips, or across lines may be illegal.

Correct parking position improves safety and traffic flow.

Drivers may stop in restricted areas if necessary because of an emergency.

Emergency stopping should only occur when absolutely necessary for safety.

Hazard lights may be used to warn other road users.

Drivers should move the vehicle to a safer location as soon as possible.

Drivers should park where the vehicle is visible and does not create danger for others.

Before leaving a parked vehicle, drivers should secure it properly.

Checking for signs, markings, and local restrictions helps avoid penalties.

Safe parking helps protect pedestrians, cyclists, and other road users.

Drivers must give way to pedestrians in situations required by road rules.

Pedestrians may be crossing at intersections, crossings, shared zones, or slip lanes.

Drivers should slow down and remain alert near pedestrian areas.

Protecting pedestrians is an important part of road safety.

Children can behave unpredictably near roads and crossings.

Drivers must take extra care near schools, playgrounds, and school crossings.

School zones operate at reduced speed limits during signed times.

Drivers should be prepared to stop quickly when children are nearby.

Cyclists are legitimate road users and must be treated with care and respect.

Drivers should check carefully for cyclists before turning, opening doors, or changing lanes.

Cyclists may need to move within the lane to avoid hazards.

Drivers should allow cyclists enough space to travel safely.

Bicycle lanes are reserved primarily for cyclists.

Drivers must obey signs and markings relating to bicycle lanes.

Before crossing or entering a bicycle lane, drivers should check carefully for cyclists.

Extra caution is required near intersections and turning points.

Shared paths are used by pedestrians, cyclists, and other approved users.

Drivers crossing shared paths should watch carefully for approaching users.

Cyclists and pedestrians may travel at different speeds on shared paths.

Awareness and caution help reduce conflicts and collisions.

Elderly and disabled pedestrians may require more time to cross roads safely.

Drivers should remain patient and prepared to stop when necessary.

Mobility aids, wheelchairs, and assistance devices may affect pedestrian speed and movement.

Extra care helps protect vulnerable road users.

Drivers must leave sufficient space when overtaking cyclists.

A safe passing distance reduces the risk of collisions and intimidation.

Drivers should only overtake cyclists when it is safe and legal to do so.

Careful observation and patience improve safety for all road users.

Drivers should remain alert for animals and other vulnerable road users near roads.

Wildlife, pets, and livestock may enter the road unexpectedly.

Drivers should slow down in areas where animal warning signs are displayed.

Safe driving helps reduce injury to people and animals.

Drivers should always consider the safety of vulnerable road users.

Pedestrians, cyclists, children, and motorcyclists are less protected in crashes.

Careful observation, safe speeds, and patience help reduce crash risk.

Responsible driving improves safety for everyone on the road.

Good driver awareness involves constantly checking for hazards and vulnerable road users.

Drivers should use mirrors, scan intersections, and check blind spots regularly.

Distraction or poor observation can increase crash risk.

Remaining alert helps drivers respond safely to changing road conditions.

Roadwork areas may contain workers, machinery, lane closures, and changed traffic conditions.

Drivers must obey temporary signs, speed limits, and directions from authorised traffic controllers.

Extra caution is required because road surfaces may be uneven or hazardous.

Careful driving helps protect workers and other road users.

Drivers must obey all signals, lights, bells, and boom gates at railway level crossings.

Drivers must stop when warning signals operate or when a train is approaching.

A crossing should not be entered unless there is enough room to clear it safely.

Ignoring railway crossing rules is dangerous and unlawful.

Drivers should switch on headlights when travelling through tunnels.

Stopping in a tunnel should only occur in emergencies.

Drivers must obey tunnel signs, lane signals, and speed limits.

Maintaining safe following distance is important in tunnel environments.

Drivers entering motorways should merge safely with traffic already travelling on the motorway.

Acceleration lanes help drivers match motorway traffic speed.

Drivers should plan lane changes early and maintain safe following distances.

Motorway ramps may require reduced speed due to curves or traffic conditions.

Rain, fog, wind, and storms can reduce visibility and vehicle control.

Drivers should slow down and increase following distance in poor weather.

Headlights may be needed when visibility is reduced.

Driving carefully in adverse weather helps reduce crash risk.

Sun glare can reduce visibility and make hazards difficult to see.

Drivers should slow down and use sun visors or suitable eyewear when needed.

Extra care is required during sunrise and sunset.

Reduced visibility increases stopping distance and crash risk.

Animals may enter roads unexpectedly, especially in rural or bush areas.

Drivers should slow down in areas marked with animal warning signs.

Sudden swerving may cause loss of vehicle control.

Careful observation and reduced speed improve safety around animals.

If a vehicle breaks down, drivers should move to a safe location if possible.

Hazard lights may be used to warn other road users.

Drivers and passengers should remain aware of surrounding traffic.

Emergency situations require calm decision making and safe actions.

Fog, smoke, heavy rain, and darkness can reduce visibility.

Drivers should slow down and increase following distance in low visibility conditions.

Headlights should be used appropriately to improve visibility.

Drivers must be able to stop safely within the distance they can see ahead.

Drivers may encounter unusual or changing road conditions that require extra caution.

Safe driving involves adjusting speed, observation, and behaviour to suit the situation.

Planning ahead and remaining alert help reduce risks.

Careful driving improves safety in all special road environments.

Drivers must give way to emergency vehicles displaying flashing lights or sounding sirens.

Emergency vehicles may include police, ambulance, and fire service vehicles.

Drivers should move safely to allow emergency vehicles to pass.

Remaining calm and avoiding sudden movements helps maintain safety.

Emergency vehicles may approach intersections against normal traffic flow when responding to emergencies.

Drivers should check carefully and give way when safe to do so.

Drivers must not block intersections when emergency vehicles are approaching.

Observation and caution are important near intersections involving emergency vehicles.

Police officers have legal authority to direct traffic.

Drivers must obey lawful directions given by police officers.

Police directions may override traffic lights, signs, or road markings.

Failing to obey police directions is an offence.

Drivers must stop when directed by police.

Police may require drivers to stop for checks, enforcement, or emergencies.

Drivers should stop safely and follow lawful instructions.

Failing to stop for police may result in serious penalties.

Police may conduct roadside breath and drug testing.

Drivers must comply with lawful testing requirements.

Refusing a test or failing to follow instructions is an offence.

Testing helps improve road safety and reduce impaired driving.

Police and emergency personnel may direct traffic around crashes or incidents.

Drivers must obey all lawful directions at accident scenes.

Temporary traffic arrangements may apply during emergencies.

Careful driving helps protect emergency workers and other road users.

Emergency vehicles may travel quickly or unexpectedly through traffic when responding to emergencies.

Drivers should remain alert for sirens and flashing lights.

Safe cooperation helps emergency services reach incidents quickly.

Drivers should not follow closely behind emergency vehicles.

Failing to obey emergency vehicle or police directions can result in fines, demerit points, or other penalties.

Road laws are designed to protect emergency workers and the public.

Unsafe behaviour around emergency situations increases crash risk.

Drivers are legally responsible for complying with lawful instructions.

Drivers should remain calm and alert during emergencies or unusual traffic situations.

Awareness of emergency vehicles, incidents, and changing traffic conditions improves safety.

Drivers should slow down and follow directions carefully near emergency scenes.

Safe behaviour helps protect all road users and emergency personnel.

Seatbelts help protect drivers and passengers from serious injury in a crash.

Drivers and passengers must wear seatbelts where fitted.

Seatbelts should be worn correctly and adjusted properly.

Wearing a seatbelt greatly improves safety during sudden braking or collisions.

Drivers are responsible for ensuring passengers follow seatbelt and restraint rules where required.

Children must be secured in suitable approved restraints.

Drivers should check that restraints are fitted and used correctly before driving.

Failing to meet these responsibilities may result in penalties.

Children must use approved restraints suitable for their age and size.

Child restraints include rear-facing restraints, forward-facing restraints, and booster seats.

Correct installation and adjustment are important for safety.

Using the appropriate restraint reduces the risk of injury in a crash.

Restrictions may apply to children travelling in the front seat.

Airbags can increase risk to young children if restraints are unsuitable.

Drivers should ensure front seat passengers use seatbelts correctly.

Passengers should remain seated properly while the vehicle is moving.

Passengers should behave safely and avoid distracting the driver.

Unsafe behaviour inside a vehicle can increase crash risk.

Passengers should keep body parts inside the vehicle while travelling.

Responsible passenger behaviour improves safety for everyone.

Some limited exemptions to seatbelt or restraint rules may apply in special circumstances.

Medical exemptions may require supporting documentation.

Drivers should understand any conditions attached to exemptions.

Safety remains important even when exemptions apply.

Failing to wear a seatbelt or properly restrain passengers can result in penalties.

Penalties may include fines, demerit points, or other enforcement action.

Seatbelt laws exist to reduce injury and save lives.

Drivers are responsible for complying with restraint requirements.

Seatbelts and restraints are essential safety devices.

Correct use of restraints helps protect occupants during sudden stops or crashes.

Drivers should encourage safe behaviour by all passengers.

Road safety depends on everyone using restraints properly.

Seatbelt and restraint requirements apply across different vehicle types where fitted.

Some vehicles may have specific seating or restraint arrangements.

Drivers should understand the restraint requirements for the vehicle being used.

Passengers should always use available restraints correctly.

Understanding restraint rules helps improve safety for drivers and passengers.

Drivers should regularly check that restraints and seatbelts are in good condition.

Awareness of restraint requirements reduces the risk of injury and penalties.

Safe restraint use protects all occupants in the vehicle.

Drivers must ensure their vehicle is roadworthy before driving.

A roadworthy vehicle is safe to operate and meets legal safety standards.

Unsafe or defective vehicles can increase crash risk.

Regular inspections and repairs help maintain vehicle safety.

Tyres must be in safe condition with adequate tread depth.

Correct tyre pressure improves braking, steering, and fuel efficiency.

Damaged or worn tyres can reduce grip and increase stopping distance.

Drivers should check tyres regularly for wear or defects.

Brakes are essential for controlling vehicle speed and stopping safely.

Drivers should ensure brakes are functioning properly before driving.

Faulty brakes can increase stopping distance and crash risk.

Any unusual brake behaviour should be checked immediately.

Vehicle lights and indicators help drivers see and communicate with other road users.

Drivers should ensure headlights, brake lights, indicators, and tail lights work correctly.

Faulty lights can reduce visibility and increase crash risk.

Indicators must be used correctly to signal intentions.

Drivers must maintain a clear view through the windscreen and mirrors.

Cracks, dirt, or obstructions can reduce visibility and safety.

Mirrors should be adjusted properly before driving.

Good visibility helps drivers detect hazards early.

Warning indicators alert drivers to possible vehicle problems.

Drivers should understand the meaning of dashboard warning lights.

Ignoring warning indicators may lead to mechanical failure or unsafe driving.

Vehicles should be checked promptly when warning lights appear.

Vehicles require correct fluid levels for safe operation.

Important fluids include engine oil, coolant, brake fluid, and windscreen washer fluid.

Low or contaminated fluids can damage the vehicle or reduce safety.

Regular fluid checks help maintain vehicle reliability.

Loads must be secured safely and must not obstruct the driver's view.

Loose or poorly secured loads can create hazards for other road users.

Drivers should ensure mirrors, windows, and lights remain visible.

Safe loading improves vehicle control and road safety.

Regular vehicle maintenance helps prevent breakdowns and unsafe conditions.

Drivers should follow recommended servicing schedules.

Routine checks can identify problems before they become dangerous.

Good maintenance habits improve vehicle safety and reliability.

Vehicle safety depends on proper maintenance, safe driving, and regular inspections.

Drivers are responsible for ensuring their vehicle remains safe to operate.

Attention to vehicle condition reduces crash risk.

Safe vehicles help protect drivers, passengers, and other road users.

Loads must be secured safely to prevent movement or falling during travel.

Loose loads can create serious hazards for other road users.

Drivers should check restraints and load stability before driving.

Proper load security improves vehicle control and road safety.

Vehicles and trailers must not exceed legal load and weight limits.

Excessive loads can affect braking, steering, and stability.

Drivers should understand the towing and carrying capacity of their vehicle.

Overloading a vehicle may result in penalties and increased crash risk.

Towing changes how a vehicle accelerates, brakes, and turns.

Drivers towing trailers should allow extra stopping distance and wider turning space.

The trailer must be connected securely before travel.

Careful driving helps maintain stability and control while towing.

Trailers must have required safety equipment fitted and functioning correctly.

Important equipment may include lights, indicators, tyres, chains, and brakes.

Drivers should inspect trailer equipment before driving.

Faulty trailer equipment can create serious safety risks.

Heavy vehicles require more space and longer distances to stop safely.

Drivers should avoid travelling in heavy vehicle blind spots.

Extra caution is needed when overtaking or merging near heavy vehicles.

Understanding heavy vehicle limitations improves road safety.

Vehicles carrying loads or towing trailers may handle differently.

Drivers should reduce speed and avoid sudden steering or braking.

Extra care is needed on curves, slopes, and in strong winds.

Safe driving behaviour helps maintain control of loaded vehicles.

Drivers are legally responsible for ensuring loads and trailers are safe and compliant.

Unsafe loading or towing may result in penalties and increased crash risk.

Drivers must obey all relevant towing and load rules.

Legal responsibilities help protect all road users.

Drivers towing loads or travelling near heavy vehicles should allow extra space and time.

Large vehicles may need additional room to turn or change lanes.

Road conditions can affect vehicle stability when carrying loads.

Patience and awareness improve safe road interaction.

Authorities may inspect vehicles, trailers, and loads for safety compliance.

Unsafe loading or towing can result in fines, penalties, or vehicle defects.

Road laws exist to reduce risks associated with heavy vehicles and loads.

Drivers should ensure loads and towing setups comply with legal requirements.

Safe towing and load management require planning, observation, and careful driving.

Drivers should regularly inspect loads, trailers, and vehicle condition.

Reducing speed and increasing following distance improves safety.

Responsible driving helps protect all road users when carrying loads or towing.

Different licence classes allow drivers to operate different types of vehicles.

Drivers must hold the correct licence class for the vehicle they are driving.

Licence classes may include cars, motorcycles, heavy vehicles, and other vehicle types.

Driving without the correct licence class is an offence.

Learner drivers must follow all learner licence conditions.

Learners are required to drive under supervision by an appropriately licensed driver.

Learner drivers must obey road rules and display L-plates correctly.

Safe supervised practice helps develop driving skills and experience.

Learner drivers must clearly display L-plates while driving.

L-plates help other road users identify learner drivers.

Plates should be visible from the front and rear of the vehicle as required.

Failing to display L-plates correctly may result in penalties.

Provisional drivers must comply with the conditions that apply to their licence.

Conditions may include restrictions relating to speed, passengers, alcohol, and mobile phones.

P-plates must be displayed correctly where required.

Breaking provisional licence conditions can result in penalties or licence action.

Learner and provisional drivers may be subject to stricter mobile phone rules.

Using a mobile phone while driving can distract attention from the road.

Drivers must follow the mobile phone restrictions that apply to their licence type.

Avoiding distractions helps improve road safety.

Passenger restrictions may apply to some provisional drivers.

These rules are designed to reduce distraction and crash risk for less experienced drivers.

Drivers must understand and comply with any passenger conditions attached to their licence.

Safe passenger management improves driving safety.

Learner drivers may need to complete supervised driving hours before progressing to the next licence stage.

Practice in different traffic and weather conditions helps build driving experience.

Accurate recording of driving hours may be required.

Supervised practice supports safer driving skills.

Demerit points apply when drivers commit certain traffic offences.

Accumulating too many demerit points may result in licence suspension or other penalties.

Learner and provisional drivers may have lower demerit point limits.

Safe and lawful driving helps drivers avoid penalties.

Learner and provisional drivers may need to complete tests or maintain logbooks.

Driving tests assess safe driving knowledge and skills.

Logbooks help record supervised driving experience where required.

Accurate records and preparation support successful licence progression.

Drivers must comply with all licence conditions and road rules.

Licence conditions are legal requirements and must be followed at all times.

Failing to comply may result in fines, demerit points, or licence suspension.

Responsible driving supports safer roads for everyone.

Safe driving requires knowledge, patience, observation, and responsible behaviour.

Learner and provisional drivers should continue developing safe driving habits.

Understanding and following road rules helps reduce crash risk.

Responsible drivers help create safer roads for all road users.
